questions.question: Which evidence should control novice-to-independent sequencing, retrieval, feedback, correction, retention, and transfer?
acceptanceEvidence: Consensus or synthesized research is translated into explicit curriculum rules and later tested with learners.

questions.question: Which alignment, support, usability, accessibility, assessment, and review evidence must block weak course approval?
acceptanceEvidence: Recognized course-quality frameworks map to review gates with named evidence and responsible re-review triggers.

questions.question: Which current primary authorities, authentic tasks, misconceptions, versions, exclusions, and prerequisites define each course?
acceptanceEvidence: Every course has a reviewed dossier and objective coverage map before architecture or bulk authoring.

questions.question: What complete beginner knowledge and practice depth must surround current Responsive Web Design v9 coverage?
acceptanceEvidence: Versioned external objectives map to original teaching, guided and faded practice, assessment, projects, and transfer.

questions.question: Which task-level patterns from strong learning platforms improve onboarding, practice, feedback, recovery, review, projects, and navigation?
acceptanceEvidence: Direct task audits record observed behavior, accessibility limits, learning purpose, rejected patterns, and local validation needs.

questions.question: Which single editor, language-service, runtime, simulator, isolation, and recovery design works accessibly on supported tablets and desktops?
acceptanceEvidence: Keyboard, screen-reader, touch, resource-bound, failure, persistence, recovery, and authentic-transfer flows pass observed tests through one editor path.

questions.question: Which evidence supports completion, mastery, retention, transfer, and project-readiness claims without misleading learners?
acceptanceEvidence: Each progress claim traces to persisted evidence and survives changed-case, delayed, rubric, and false-positive review.

questions.question: Can learners predict where they are, what comes next, why work is locked, how to recover, and what progress means?
acceptanceEvidence: Cognitive walkthroughs and representative learner tasks close critical labeling, wayfinding, comprehension, and recovery failures.

questions.question: Which threat, isolation, data-minimization, authorization, answer-security, AI, and ethical boundaries must every learner flow enforce?
acceptanceEvidence: Threat models and adversarial tests prove learner text cannot execute on hosts, escape bounds, expose answers, or cause external actions.

questions.question: Which current stable versions work together across install, build, tests, runtime packaging, browsers, and supported editor flows?
acceptanceEvidence: Official compatibility evidence and reproducible clean smokes pass for every major dependency and supported environment.

questions.question: Where do representative novice, intermediate, advanced, disabled, keyboard-only, and tablet learners misunderstand or fail real tasks?
acceptanceEvidence: Consent-aware task observations produce severity-rated findings, repairs, and successful re-tests without invented analytics.

sources.title: Organizing Instruction and Study to Improve Student Learning

claims.statement: Spacing, interleaving worked examples with problem solving, retrieval through quizzing, and deep explanatory questions are supported instructional practices.
limitations: Guide does not prescribe one universal interval, activity count, software interaction, or mastery formula for this platform.

triggers: IES replaces the guide or a newer high-quality synthesis materially changes these recommendations.

sources.title: How People Learn II: Learners, Contexts, and Cultures

claims.statement: Prior knowledge, structured support, retrieval, feedback, motivation, agency, context, and transfer interact in learning design.
limitations: Broad synthesis informs design hypotheses but does not validate any specific LEARN-IT-ALL lesson or interface without learner evidence.

triggers: National Academies publishes a superseding consensus report relevant to learning and educational technology.

claims.statement: Multiple means of engagement, representation, and action or expression should reduce avoidable barriers while preserving meaningful goals.
limitations: Framework guides barrier analysis; it does not make every representation equivalent or replace WCAG conformance and direct usability testing.

triggers: CAST publishes a new UDL guideline version or implementation guidance.

claims.statement: Course overview, objectives, assessment, materials, activities, technology, learner support, accessibility, and usability require coherent alignment.
limitations: Public framework overview informs internal gates but this project does not claim official Quality Matters review, certification, or accreditation.

triggers: Quality Matters releases a new higher-education rubric edition.

sources.title: Open SUNY Course Quality Review Rubric

claims.statement: Predictable course information, navigation, interaction, technology, support, accessibility, and continuous review are explicit online-course quality concerns.
limitations: Rubric supports structured review but automated checks cannot substitute for reviewer judgment or observed learner success.

triggers: SUNY revises OSCQR standards or publishes a new rubric version.

sources.title: ACM IEEE-CS AAAI Computer Science Curricula 2023

claims.statement: Computing curricula should connect knowledge areas, competencies, professional dispositions, authentic context, and program-level coherence.
limitations: Undergraduate curriculum guidance must be adapted to each course audience and cannot supply detailed learner-facing sequence by itself.

triggers: ACM, IEEE-CS, or AAAI publishes a superseding computing curriculum report.

claims.statement: Current certification structure supplies a concrete external breadth and practice-depth benchmark across lectures, workshops, labs, reviews, quizzes, projects, and examination.
limitations: External names and counts do not prove pedagogical quality and cannot be copied as learner-facing explanations, scenarios, code, checks, or solutions.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes v9 structure, objectives, certification projects, or reference challenge inventory.

claims.statement: MDN defines a standards-oriented set of essential front-end knowledge, practices, accessibility expectations, testing, and collaboration outcomes.
limitations: Curriculum describes scope and outcomes; LEARN-IT-ALL must still author, sequence, practice, assess, and validate original instruction.

triggers: MDN revises curriculum modules, outcomes, or Learn Web Development structure.

claims.statement: Keyboard, focus, status, target size, reflow, input, semantics, contrast, and motion requirements apply across supported learner flows.
limitations: Conformance checks do not prove overall usability, instructional clarity, editor accessibility, or successful assistive-technology use.

triggers: W3C publishes a superseding WCAG Recommendation or applicable normative correction.

sources.title: CodeMirror 6 Documentation, Tab Handling, and Changelog
sources.versionOrPublished: CodeMirror 6 package set current through @codemirror/view 6.43.6; official docs and changelog reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: CodeMirror 6 provides a composable browser editor, intentionally leaves Tab unbound to avoid a keyboard trap, exposes content attributes for accessible labeling, and records current touch, mobile composition, screen-reader announcement, selection, and diagnostic repairs in its official changelog.
limitations: Documentation and package release notes do not prove this integration is usable on the supported tablet, desktop, browser, keyboard, touch, zoom, high-contrast, or assistive-technology matrix; direct observed tests remain required.

triggers: CodeMirror changes view, state, language, accessibility, input, browser, or integration behavior.

sources.title: Exercism Learning Mode and syllabus documentation

claims.statement: A visible concept prerequisite graph can unlock focused practice while optional practice mode and mentoring preserve learner agency.
limitations: Official documentation describes product behavior, not comparative learning outcomes; LEARN-IT-ALL must test prerequisite explanations, bridge routes, bypasses, and recovery with its own learners.

triggers: Exercism changes learning mode, concept prerequisites, exercise unlocking, mentoring, or practice-mode behavior.

sources.title: The Odin Project learning roadmap and Foundations guidance

claims.statement: A visible cumulative roadmap with strategically placed projects makes the learning destination and build progression concrete.
limitations: The roadmap does not establish that every project has sufficient formative feedback, accessibility support, safe execution, or valid mastery evidence for this platform.

triggers: The Odin Project changes its roadmap, project progression, Foundations sequencing, or learner support model.

sources.title: Carnegie Mellon Open Learning Initiative practice pages

claims.statement: Low-stakes practice with immediate feedback should let learners test and correct understanding before graded assessment.
limitations: Product guidance supports a sequence hypothesis but does not validate LEARN-IT-ALL feedback wording, hint progression, retry policy, or assessment reliability.

triggers: OLI changes practice-page, feedback, hint, assessment, or learning-engine guidance.

claims.statement: Delayed mixed review can revisit eligible skills, and later evidence can lower a previously higher mastery level.
limitations: This is product behavior rather than evidence that the same schedule or scoring model is valid for coding, design, debugging, projects, or transfer.

triggers: Khan Academy changes mastery levels, challenge scheduling, eligibility, review composition, or progress behavior.

claims.statement: Named milestones can group lessons, quizzes, practice, and projects into a legible route toward a larger learner goal.
limitations: Milestone clarity does not make completion a competence claim; evidence strength, prerequisite reasoning, remediation, retention, and transfer still need independent validation.

triggers: Codecademy changes path structure, milestones, project placement, prerequisites, or progress presentation.

claims.statement: A composable multi-file editor, browser bundler, preview, dependency surface, and error feedback can shorten the build-observe-revise loop.
limitations: A third-party component does not prove accessibility, deterministic grading, offline behavior, dependency safety, resource isolation, persistence, or suitability for non-JavaScript domains.

triggers: Sandpack changes editor, bundler, package execution, browser support, accessibility, or security behavior.

claims.statement: Running Pyodide in a dedicated worker separates its global scope from the document and prevents long Python computation from directly blocking the interface thread.
limitations: A worker is an availability and compartment boundary, not a complete security sandbox; Web APIs, package loading, memory, output, persistence, and message validation remain explicit threats.

triggers: Pyodide changes worker guidance, Web API access, package loading, browser support, or runtime behavior.

claims.statement: Sandbox tokens selectively relax restrictions, so learner preview capability must be minimized and same-origin plus script combinations treated as security-sensitive.
limitations: The standard defines browser behavior but does not prove a platform policy is complete, consistently enforced, or safe against every browser defect and application data flow.

triggers: WHATWG changes sandbox tokens, origin behavior, navigation, downloads, permissions, or embedded-content security requirements.

claims.statement: A worker can be terminated immediately, enabling an outer controller to enforce a wall-clock execution limit and replace the runtime after timeout.
limitations: Termination does not by itself bound allocations before timeout, validate messages, prevent network-capable APIs, preserve state safely, or prove cleanup across browsers.

triggers: Worker lifecycle, termination, browser support, or relevant isolation guidance changes.

claims.statement: After the current dependency and runtime purge, npm ls reports a valid tree. npm outdated reports TypeScript 7.0.2 as newer than the selected 6.0.3, but TypeScript 7 ships without the programmatic API required by current Next.js tooling; the tested stack therefore records this one explicit compatibility exception.
limitations: A current registry comparison and valid local dependency tree do not prove clean installation, runtime behavior, browser behavior, or compatibility on every supported operating system.

triggers: A direct or transitive dependency changes, a peer constraint changes, a security advisory appears, or a supported runtime or browser changes.

claims.statement: TypeScript 7.0 does not ship a programmatic API; the TypeScript team expects a new API in 7.1 and documents TypeScript 6 for tools that still need programmatic compiler access.
limitations: The announcement does not certify Next.js compatibility, define a browser learner-runtime design, or authorize sending learner source to a host compiler. Local Next.js type-generation behavior is separate direct evidence.

triggers: TypeScript 7.1 or another stable release exposes the new API, Next.js documents TypeScript 7 support, or the isolated learner-runtime design changes.

decisions.title: Require cumulative novice-to-transfer progression
decisions.rationale: Research supports modeling, guided and faded practice, retrieval, correction, delayed use, and transfer as connected evidence rather than isolated content inventory.
validation: Coverage and prerequisite audits plus observed learner transfer tasks must pass before course approval.

decisions.title: Separate completion, mastery, retention, and transfer
decisions.rationale: Activity completion alone cannot support a durable competence claim; progress must name evidence strength, recency, changed cases, correction, and transfer.
validation: False-positive grading review and learner comprehension tests must prove every displayed metric means what its label claims.

decisions.title: Block approval on whole-course alignment and access
decisions.rationale: Objectives, assessments, materials, interaction, technology, learner support, accessibility, usability, and review evidence must work as one course system.
validation: Subject, instructional, assessment, accessibility, safety, and observed-learner reviews must close critical findings.

decisions.title: Research scope before module sequence
decisions.rationale: Course headings and generated quantities cannot replace current authoritative scope, observable competencies, prerequisites, authentic tasks, misconceptions, and aligned evidence.
validation: Every external objective must trace through competency, teaching, practice, assessment, project, and maintenance evidence.

decisions.title: Match RWD breadth and depth with original instruction
decisions.rationale: freeCodeCamp v9 gives a concrete certification coverage benchmark while standards and MDN supply technical authority; learner-facing work remains wholly original.
validation: Versioned objective mapping, duplication review, meaningful step audit, real grading, and beginner learner pilots must pass.

decisions.title: Support tablet and desktop studios through one verified editor
decisions.rationale: Course editors require room and keyboard-capable interaction. CodeMirror 6 is the only coding editor path, supported tablet and desktop environments must pass the same gates, and phones receive a clear handoff rather than a second implementation.
validation: Keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, reflow, touch, reduced-motion, diagnostics, persistence, recovery, and phone-handoff tasks must pass through the one supported editor path.

decisions.title: Use transparent prerequisite unlocks with corrective choice
decisions.rationale: A concept graph can protect learning order without becoming a dead end when every lock explains the missing evidence and offers a diagnostic, bridge, review, or justified practice bypass.
validation: Novice and returning learners must correctly predict why an item is unavailable and reach an appropriate corrective path without assistance.

decisions.title: Make cumulative projects visible across the roadmap
decisions.rationale: Learners need to see how each module changes a real artifact and prepares a later independent project, while project checks remain formative until the learner must transfer without a recipe.
validation: Learners must explain the relationship between current work and later projects, then complete an unfamiliar transfer project without copied workshop structure.

decisions.title: Require feedback and correction before summative evidence
decisions.rationale: Learners need low-stakes attempts, causal feedback, graduated hints, revision, and a parallel retry before a high-stakes result can defensibly represent their current understanding.
validation: Assessment audits must reject summative checks that introduce new skills, leak answers, lack correction, or repeat the practice case without meaningful change.

decisions.title: Reassess retained skill with delayed mixed work
decisions.rationale: A completed activity remains historical evidence, while current retention should update from later mixed tasks and may decline when a learner can no longer perform the skill reliably.
validation: Pilots must show that learners understand why completion stays recorded while retention changes, and delayed tasks must discriminate remembered performance from recognition.

decisions.title: Make milestones trace to real evidence
decisions.rationale: Course maps should provide legible milestones without turning activity counts into mastery; every status label opens the persisted attempt, competency, review, project, or transfer evidence behind it.
validation: Representative learners must interpret each metric correctly and every displayed value must reconcile with persisted source evidence under changed and failed attempts.

decisions.title: Keep build, observation, diagnosis, and revision together
decisions.rationale: A cohesive multi-file workspace can reduce interaction cost and make cause and effect visible when output, diagnostics, evidence, hints, and revision remain accessible without solving the task for the learner.
validation: Tablet, desktop, keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, failure, persistence, and changed-case learner tasks must pass with no solution leakage or focus loss.

decisions.title: Treat browser workers as disposable bounded runtimes
decisions.rationale: Workers keep heavy execution off the interface thread and can be replaced after timeout, but security requires fresh instances, strict message admission, allowlists, output and state limits, network denial, and explicit transfer boundaries.
validation: Adversarial tests must prove timeout replacement, message validation, output limits, denied capabilities, clean state, accessible failure recovery, and no learner source reaches the host.

decisions.title: Render web artifacts in opaque minimum-capability previews
decisions.rationale: Learner-authored web content needs an origin and capability boundary that withholds same-origin authority, navigation, downloads, storage, credentials, and unnecessary browser permissions while still supporting bounded observable behavior.
validation: Security tests must attempt parent access, top navigation, downloads, storage, credentials, popups, network effects, output flooding, and sandbox mutation across supported browsers.

decisions.title: Accept upgrades only after dependency and repository smokes
decisions.rationale: Registry latest status is useful inventory, but compatibility is established only when peer constraints resolve and the reproducible install, package tree, tests, types, lint, build, runtime packaging, and changed browser flows all pass.
validation: Run npm outdated, npm ls, audit, clean-install review, full non-Lighthouse gates, and supported browser flows after every dependency wave; record exceptions and rollback boundaries.
